meta.title: List
meta.description: The list component organizes related items or data into a vertical, scannable format.

toc.value: Nested items, added gap space, and item wrapping

mdx:
## Overview

The list component presents structured content which can include details such as
text, icons, and actions, in a vertical arrangement. With improved readability
letting readers quickly scan the content and compare relevant details.

### Resources

Deep dive into implementation details and access the Nimbus design library.

[W3C: Lists](https://www.w3.org/WAI/tutorials/page-structure/content/#lists)
[Chakra UI: List](https://chakra-ui.com/docs/components/list)

## Variables

Get familiar with the features.

### Basic options

#### Vertical lists

Vertical lists can function differently based on need. Using bullet points,
numbered lists, and icons or emojis will help users understand the information
differently as needed.

```jsx live
const App = () => (
  <Stack direction="horizontal" gap="800" alignItems="flex-start">
    <List.Root>
      <List.Item>Red pandas</List.Item>
      <List.Item>Pangolins</List.Item>
      <List.Item>Opossums</List.Item>
      <List.Item>Aye-ayes</List.Item>
      <List.Item>Okapi</List.Item>
    </List.Root>
    <List.Root as="ol">
      <List.Item>Boil water</List.Item>
      <List.Item>Add pasta</List.Item>
      <List.Item>Wait patiently to cook</List.Item>
      <List.Item>Remove and drain pasta</List.Item>
      <List.Item>Add final ingredients and serve</List.Item>
    </List.Root>
    <List.Root variant="plain">
      <List.Item>
        <List.Indicator>
          <Icon color="positive.9">
            <Icons.Circle />
          </Icon>
        </List.Indicator>
        Reading a book
      </List.Item>
      <List.Item>
        <List.Indicator>
          <Icon color="positive.9">
            <Icons.Circle />
          </Icon>
        </List.Indicator>
        Talking to a friend
      </List.Item>
      <List.Item>
        <List.Indicator>
          <Icon color="positive.9">
            <Icons.Circle />
          </Icon>
        </List.Indicator>
        Going for a walk
      </List.Item>
      <List.Item>
        <List.Indicator>
          <Icon color="critical.11"><Icons.Close /></Icon>
        </List.Indicator>
        Doomscrolling
      </List.Item>
      <List.Item>
        <List.Indicator>
          <Icon color="critical.11"><Icons.Close /></Icon>
        </List.Indicator>
        Not eating a treat
      </List.Item>
    </List.Root>
  </Stack>
)
```

#### Alignment

List items are aligned to the left, center and the right. Choose what suits the
information to be shown based on language and data.

```jsx live
const App = () => (
  <Stack direction="horizontal" gap="600" alignItems="flex-start">
    <Stack gap="200" flex="1">
      <Text fontWeight="bold" textAlign="left">Left</Text>
      <List.Root>
        <List.Item textAlign="left">Red pandas</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="left">Pangolins</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="left">Opossums</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="left">Aye-ayes</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="left">Okapi</List.Item>
      </List.Root>
    </Stack>
    <Stack gap="200" flex="1">
      <Text fontWeight="bold" textAlign="center">Center</Text>
      <List.Root>
        <List.Item textAlign="center">Red pandas</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="center">Pangolins</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="center">Opossums</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="center">Aye-ayes</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="center">Okapi</List.Item>
      </List.Root>
    </Stack>
    <Stack gap="200" flex="1">
      <Text fontWeight="bold" textAlign="right">Right</Text>
      <List.Root>
        <List.Item textAlign="right">Red pandas</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="right">Pangolins</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="right">Opossums</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="right">Aye-ayes</List.Item>
        <List.Item textAlign="right">Okapi</List.Item>
      </List.Root>
    </Stack>
  </Stack>
)
```

#### Nested items, added gap space, and item wrapping

When nesting related items, maintain proper spacing and wrapping to keep the
structure intact. Avoid long lines of text; if necessary, rephrase to make it
shorter or use body text instead of bullet points, which should be reserved for
concise information.

```jsx live
const App = () => (
  <Stack direction="horizontal" gap="800" alignItems="flex-start">
    <Stack gap="200">
      <Text fontWeight="bold">Nested items</Text>
      <List.Root>
        <List.Item>
          Password requirements:
          <List.Root pl="400">
            <List.Item>8 characters and 3 numbers</List.Item>
            <List.Item>One special character (!@#$%^&*)</List.Item>
          </List.Root>
        </List.Item>
        <List.Item>
          Name and email requirements
          <List.Root pl="400">
            <List.Item>Validation process</List.Item>
          </List.Root>
        </List.Item>
      </List.Root>
    </Stack>
    <Stack gap="200">
      <Text fontWeight="bold">Gap space</Text>
      <List.Root gap="400">
        <List.Item>
          Password requirements:
          <List.Root pl="400">
            <List.Item>8 characters and 3 numbers</List.Item>
            <List.Item>One special character (!@#$%^&*)</List.Item>
          </List.Root>
        </List.Item>
        <List.Item>
          Name and email requirements
          <List.Root pl="400">
            <List.Item>Validation process</List.Item>
          </List.Root>
        </List.Item>
      </List.Root>
    </Stack>
    <Stack gap="200">
      <Text fontWeight="bold">Item wrapping</Text>
      <List.Root maxWidth="200px">
        <List.Item>
          Password requirements:
          <List.Root pl="400">
            <List.Item>8 characters and 3 numbers</List.Item>
            <List.Item>One special character (!@#$%^&*)</List.Item>
          </List.Root>
        </List.Item>
        <List.Item>
          Name and email requirements
          <List.Root pl="400">
            <List.Item>Validation process</List.Item>
          </List.Root>
        </List.Item>
      </List.Root>
    </Stack>
  </Stack>
)
```

a11y.mdx:
## Accessibility

Accessibility ensures that digital content and functionality are usable by
everyone, including people with disabilities, by addressing visual, auditory,
cognitive, and physical limitations.

```jsx live
const App = () => (
  <List.Root>
    <List.Item>
      Password requirements:
      <List.Root pl="400">
        <List.Item>8 characters and 3 numbers</List.Item>
        <List.Item>One special character (!@#$%^&*)</List.Item>
      </List.Root>
    </List.Item>
    <List.Item>
      Name and email requirements
      <List.Root pl="400">
        <List.Item>Validation process</List.Item>
      </List.Root>
    </List.Item>
  </List.Root>
)
```

### Accessibility standards

- **Keyboard navigation:** Ensure all interactive list items are navigable using
  the Tab key and fully operable using the Enter or Spacebar keys.
- **ARIA roles:** Use semantic HTML (`<ul>`, `<ol>`) when possible. For custom
  interactive lists, use appropriate ARIA roles (e.g., `role="listbox"`,
  `role="option"`) to define the component's structure.
- **Focus indicators:** Provide a strong, clear visual focus indicator on the
  entire list item when it is being accessed via the keyboard.
- **Text alternatives:** Ensure any icons or custom elements within the list
  item have appropriate aria-label or descriptive text.
- **Reading order:** The programmatic reading order must logically match the
  visual vertical order.

guidelines.mdx:
## Guidelines

List guidelines aim to provide users the best experience when understanding
information that is being conveyed through the interface.

### Best practices

- **Consistency in content:** Ensure all items within a single list adhere to
  the same content structure (e.g., if one item has an icon and metadata, all
  items should).
- **Clear hierarchy:** Use typography, indentation, and visual separators to
  establish a clear hierarchy between list items and their sub-elements.
- **Easy scanning:** Prioritize short, primary information at the beginning of
  each list item to enable quick scanning.
- **Interactivity:** Clearly distinguish interactive list items
  (tappable/clickable) from static, informational ones using visual cues like
  hover states and pointers.
- **State management:** Define consistent visual states for list items,
  including default, hover, focused, selected, and disabled.
- **Grouping:** Use dividers, headers, or subtle visual spacing to group related
  items within a long list (e.g., grouping customers by status or date).
- **Limited information:** Avoid cramming too much detail into a single list
  item; use secondary detail panels, cards, or dedicated pages for complex
  information.

### Usage

Lists are fundamental UI components used to display a series of related items,
allowing users to quickly scan, organize, and interact with structured data or
options.

> [!TIP]\
> When to use

- **Navigation menus:** For primary or secondary menus where items are primarily
  text links.
- **Short metadata/tasks:** For displaying a history log, task list, or brief
  metadata where the vertical format is optimal for chronological or simple item
  tracking.
- **Structured options:** To present settings, filters, or menu choices where
  hierarchy and text are the main content.

> [!CAUTION]\
> When not to use

- **Image-heavy content:** For displaying a collection where the visual preview
  (e.g., product image) is the most critical element.
- **Wide data sets:** When the content requires many columns of data that won't
  fit on a single line, even on a large screen.
- **Complex data comparison:** When the user needs to compare many different
  data points across items, use a data table instead.

### Do's and don'ts for implementing a list

> [!TIP]\
> **Do**
>
> - Ensure adequate padding and spacing to clearly separate each list item.
> - Provide clear feedback when an item is selected or activated.
> - Use semantic HTML (`<ul>`, `<ol>`, `<li>`) to convey structure to assistive
>   technologies.
> - Allow content within the list item to truncate gracefully, revealing the
>   full text via a tooltip or expansion interactive.

> [!CAUTION]\
> **Don't**
>
> - Don't use generic links like "Click here"; make the entire interactive item
>   text descriptive.
> - Don't mix vastly different content structures or types of items in one list.
> - Don't neglect a strategy for empty states (what happens when the list has no
>   items?).
> - Don't use low-contrast text for critical list item details.

toc.value: Do's and don'ts for implementing a list
